title: Prestonia cayennensis
taxonomy: Prestonia cayennensis is classified within the family Apocynaceae, commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. This family comprises over 5,000 species of flowering plants, many of which are native to tropical and subtropical regions around the world. Prestonia cayennensis is further classified within the genus Prestonia, which is a group of flowering plants native to Central and South America. The genus Prestonia is characterized by its showy flowers and milky sap. As a member of the Apocynaceae family, Prestonia cayennensis has a unique set of characteristics that distinguish it from other plant species.
uses: Prestonia cayennensis is a plant species used in traditional medicine, particularly in South American folk medicine. Its extracts have been studied for their potential antimalarial and anti-inflammatory properties. The plant is also used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory issues. In some regions, Prestonia cayennensis is used to reduce blood pressure and as an anti-diarrheal agent. Further research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal uses and potential applications.

intro: Prestonia cayennensis, also known as Cayenne jasmine or Cayenne root, is a plant species belonging to the Apocynaceae family, native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its purported health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiseptic properties, which may aid in wound healing, reducing fever, and combating infections. The bioactive constituents of Prestonia cayennensis include alkaloids, glycosides, and iridoids, such as cayenne jasmine alkaloid, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Prestonia cayennensis is commonly prepared as a tincture, tea, or infusion, and is used to treat a variety of ailments, including fever, cough, and respiratory issues.
